topics.rationale: This is the server-side core and the only C# production code, so it is distinct from all other topics. It carries several concrete targets the task names. Duplicate code: FilesController.cs repeats an identical 5-catch block (ArgumentException, UnauthorizedAccessException, DirectoryNotFoundException, FileNotFoundException, IOException) across all six endpoints (~30 catches); the last two are IOException subtypes, so those catches are redundant ordering. Dead code: the injected `_logger` field is stored but never referenced. Performance: `new FileExtensionContentTypeProvider()` is instantiated on every Download request instead of cached; Browse does two directory enumerations (GetDirectories + GetFiles) where EnumerateFileSystemEntries would suffice. Bugs/hardening: Move/Copy use `Path.GetDirectoryName(destination)!` null-forgiving (throws when destination has no parent); the singleton FileService does check-then-act (Directory.Exists then Delete) TOCTOU; Upload returns a path built from the raw, unnormalized `path` query parameter. Files: Controllers/FilesController.cs, Services/FileService.cs, Services/IFileService.cs, Models/FileSystemModels.cs, Program.cs, FileServiceOptions.cs, appsettings.json, appsettings.Development.json, TestProject.csproj

The client-side app and the only owner of src/*.ts (non-test) and the stylesheet, so it does not overlap the test topics. Monolith: app.ts (472 lines) mixes DOM construction, event wiring, route dispatch, and every table/row/breadcrumb builder — the task explicitly asks to break monoliths into domain modules (e.g. dom-builders, render, toolbar-handlers). Dead code: parseHash, parentPath, and basename are imported into app.ts but never used; renderBrowse/renderSearch accept a `route` parameter they discard via `void route`. Memory leak: `subscribe(render)` throws away the returned unsubscribe function, so the hashchange listener and module-level DOM refs accumulate across remounts. Dead/mismatched CSS: `.folder-row`, `.breadcrumb .separator`, and the entire `.toolbar label.upload-btn` block target classes the TS never emits (the upload label uses `btn`, the separator span has no class). Magic values: the literal 1024 in formatBytes. Files: src/app.ts, src/api.ts, src/router.ts, src/format.ts, src/app.css, wwwroot/index.html

Owns the *.test.ts files exclusively (disjoint from production sources and from the C# tests), addressing the task's dead-test and tautological-test targets on the JS side. Tautological tests: api.test.ts's 'shared response types' describe block just assigns object literals and asserts the same values back. Monolith test file: app.test.ts is 940 lines and can be split by concern (structure, renderBrowse, renderSearch, orchestration, toolbar). Duplicate code: the `mockResponse` helper is copy-pasted between api.test.ts and app.test.ts. Superfluous-feature test: readme.deep-linking.test.ts asserts on README prose rather than code behavior, a candidate for removal. Files: src/app.test.ts, src/api.test.ts, src/format.test.ts, src/router.test.ts, src/router.dom.test.ts, src/app.css.test.ts, src/index.html.test.ts, src/readme.deep-linking.test.ts, vitest.config.ts

Owns the TestProject.Tests/** C# specs exclusively (disjoint from production code and JS tests), covering the task's dead-test, tautological-test, and superfluous-feature targets on the .NET side. Brittle/superfluous: ProgramWiringTests (367 lines) parses Program.cs source via regex to assert on DI wiring and 'template preservation' — it tests source text rather than behavior, and the scaffolding-preservation tests guard against a template that no longer exists. Tautological: FileSystemModelsTests asserts compiler-generated record features (value equality, sealed, property types, that arrays/lists satisfy IReadOnlyList); FileServiceOptionsTests asserts a trivial auto-property getter/setter. Duplication: IFileServiceContractTests and FilesControllerContractTests reflection-probe method signatures already exercised by the behavioral FilesControllerTests/FileServiceTests. Files: TestProject.Tests/Services/FileServiceTests.cs, TestProject.Tests/Services/IFileServiceContractTests.cs, TestProject.Tests/Models/FileSystemModelsTests.cs, TestProject.Tests/Controllers/FilesControllerTests.cs, TestProject.Tests/Controllers/FilesControllerContractTests.cs, TestProject.Tests/FileServiceOptionsTests.cs, TestProject.Tests/ProgramWiringTests.cs, TestProject.Tests/TestProject.Tests.csproj

## Synthesized Research Summary

### Files Examined
- **Backend production**: Controllers/FilesController.cs, Services/FileService.cs, FileServiceOptions.cs, Program.cs, appsettings.json, appsettings.Development.json
- **Frontend production**: src/app.ts, src/app.css, src/router.ts, src/format.ts, src/api.ts
- **Backend tests**: FileServiceTests.cs, IFileServiceContractTests.cs, FileSystemModelsTests.cs, FilesControllerTests.cs, FilesControllerContractTests.cs, FileServiceOptionsTests.cs, ProgramWiringTests.cs
- **Frontend tests**: src/app.test.ts, src/api.test.ts, src/router.test.ts, src/router.dom.test.ts, src/format.test.ts, src/readme.deep-linking.test.ts, vitest.config.ts

### Key Findings

**Dead Code (production)**: 
- Backend: Unused `_logger` field in FilesController; unused `using` directives
- Frontend: 3 unused imports in app.ts (parseHash, parentPath, basename); 3 dead CSS classes (`.folder-row`, `.separator`, `.upload-btn`)

**Dead / Tautological Tests (~76 tests, ~900 lines)**:
- FileSystemModelsTests.cs (28 tests) — tests compiler-guaranteed record features
- FileServiceOptionsTests.cs (6 tests) — tests trivial auto-property behavior
- IFileServiceContractTests.cs (12 tests) — reflection-based interface signature checks (compiler-enforced)
- FilesControllerContractTests.cs (~30 tests) — reflection-based controller attribute/signature checks (compiler-enforced)
- Frontend: "shared response types" block in api.test.ts (3 tests); readme.deep-linking.test.ts (tests README prose, not code)
- ProgramWiringTests template-preservation tests guard extinct scaffolding

**Monolith Files**:
- `src/app.ts` (472 lines) — mixes DOM construction, route dispatch, toolbar handlers, browse/search rendering, DOM builder helpers → split into domain modules
- `src/app.test.ts` (940 lines) — covers 6 distinct concerns → split by domain
- `TestProject.Tests/Controllers/FilesControllerTests.cs` (~558 lines) — embeds FakeFileService (80+ lines) inline → extract

**Duplicate Code**:
- Backend: Identical 5-catch blocks duplicated across 6 controller endpoints (~210 lines); extract to helper/middleware
- Backend tests: `CreateFormFile` helper duplicated in FileServiceTests and FilesControllerTests; CaughtExceptions pattern repeated 7×
- Frontend tests: `mockResponse` helper duplicated in api.test.ts and app.test.ts

**Bugs**:
- Backend: Null-forgiving operator on `Path.GetDirectoryName()` can crash on root paths; TOCTOU race in Delete (check-then-act); unnormalized response paths from raw query parameter
- Frontend: Memory leak from discarded `subscribe()` unsubscribe handle (hashchange listeners accumulate); race condition in `render()` with no deduplication/abort; unhandled promise rejections in action button handlers; missing error handling in upload loop

**Performance**:
- `FileExtensionContentTypeProvider` instantiated per request → cache static
- `Directory.GetDirectories` + `Directory.GetFiles` walk directory twice → single pass

**Code Smells**:
- `FileService` constructor creates home directory during DI resolution (startup failure risk); no null-check on `IFormFile`; recursive `CopyDirectory` with no depth limit; `ComputeParent` uses manual string ops instead of `Path.GetDirectoryName`
- Frontend: `void route;` workaround for unused parameters; module-level DOM refs create stale-closure race; fragile `leafByText` test helper
- `FileServiceOptions` lives in root namespace (inconsistent)

**Magic Values**:
- `MaxSearchResults = 500` hardcoded; 1024 inline in formatBytes; ISO_FMT expected string in tests stales independently; 550 magic buffer count in search-cap test

### Summary
The codebase is moderate-sized (frontend SPA + backend .NET Web API). All 10 improvement categories have identified issues. The most impactful changes are: (1) deleting ~900 lines of tautological/contract tests, (2) splitting monolith files (app.ts, app.test.ts, FilesControllerTests.cs), (3) fixing the hashchange listener memory leak and render race condition in the frontend, (4) refactoring the duplicated catch-block pattern in the controller, and (5) addressing the TOCTOU race and null-handling bugs in FileService.

output.feedback: The plan is sound and comprehensive. All 29 tasks are concrete, correctly scoped, and have valid dependency ordering. One MEDIUM finding: tasks 20, 21, and 22 all modify src/app.ts but have no inter-dependencies, yet the strategy states 'Within each phase tasks are parallelizable' — if the execution system runs them truly in parallel, file-level conflicts will occur. Recommend adding sequential dependencies (task-21 depends on task-20; task-22 depends on task-21) or explicitly noting these three must execute sequentially within Phase 3 despite being in the same phase.
suggestions: MEDIUM: Add dependencies so tasks 20, 21, 22 (all editing src/app.ts) execute sequentially. The strategy's claim that same-phase tasks are parallelizable conflicts with these same-file tasks. Suggested: task-21 depends on task-20, task-22 depends on task-21.

LOW: Task-12 says 'The lazy Root accessor added by task-16 (if landed)' — the parenthetical '(if landed)' introduces unnecessary uncertainty since task-12 explicitly depends on task-16. Simplify to 'The lazy Root accessor added by task-16'.

LOW: Task-27 lists multiple alternative approaches for excluding test-utils from the production build (separate tsconfig, __test-utils__ directory, exclude array). Recommend picking one concrete approach (e.g., add 'src/test-utils/**' to the tsconfig exclude array) rather than leaving the choice ambiguous.

LOW: The mockResponse helper differs between api.test.ts and app.test.ts — api.test.ts has 'json: async () => (opts.body ?? (opts.text ? JSON.parse(opts.text) : {}))' while app.test.ts has 'json: async () => (opts.body ?? {})'. Task-27 says 'pick the union of both behaviors' but should explicitly state to use the more permissive api.test.ts version so all existing call sites continue to work.

LOW: The research identified several minor code smells not addressed by any task: MaxSearchResults = 500 hardcoded constant (already named, acceptable), 1024 inline in formatBytes, 550 magic number in Search_CapsResultsAtFiveHundred test, and the fragile leafByText test helper. These are all LOW impact and acceptable omissions given the task's latitude.

The test file (src/app.test.ts) is well-written and comprehensive. The 5 failing tests are intentionally failing — they are TDD tests that define the expected behavior for the error handling wrapper that has not yet been implemented. The test suite demonstrates strong quality across all review criteria:

**Specificity & focus:** Each test in the 'error surfacing (rejecting handlers)' sub-describe targets exactly one behavior: error display for a specific action, error type, or success path.

**Edge-case coverage:** Excellent — covers all three mutation actions (Delete, Move, Copy) with API rejections at different HTTP status codes (400, 409, 500), non-Error rejections (thrown strings), the success path (verifying no error is shown), and user cancellation (confirm/prompt returning false/null).

**Test patterns:** Consistently follows the project's established patterns: `setup()`/`setupCleared()` for fresh DOM, `flush()` for async settling, `fetchMock` for API stubbing, `vi.spyOn` for browser APIs (confirm/prompt), and `mockResponse` helper.

**Mock design:** The `failMutation` helper is well-designed — it stubs specific mutation endpoints to return errors while leaving all other fetch calls (browse, search) permissive, preventing false failures from background renders.

**Independence:** All tests are fully independent — each calls `actionsForFile()` which internally calls `setup()`, creating a fresh DOM context.

**No tautological tests:** Every test has meaningful assertions. The 'surfaces the error in .status only' test is particularly valuable as a regression guard, verifying that errors are isolated to the status footer and don't corrupt the results table.

**No dead/disabled tests:** No `test.skip`, `xtest`, commented-out, or empty tests.

**No issues found.** The test suite is ready to serve as the TDD specification for the implementation.

The 6 characterization tests are well-written, follow existing patterns, and provide meaningful edge-case coverage for the retained imports. Five of the six tests (normalizeRelativePath, formatBytes, formatDate, toBrowseHash, toSearchHash) add genuine incremental value with new edge cases not covered by the existing suite. All assertions are meaningful and non-tautological. The one minor concern is that the joinPath breadcrumb test (line 973) is a near-duplicate of the existing breadcrumb test at line 667 — same setup/teardown pattern and assertion style, differing only in 3-level vs 2-level paths — so its incremental value is marginal. This is a minor issue, not a reason to block. The tests are ready to accompany the implementation change (removing parseHash, parentPath, and basename from src/app.ts imports).

issues.description: The 'joinPath (breadcrumb cumulative paths)' test (line 973) is a near-duplicate of the existing 'renders clickable segments that navigate to each cumulative path' test (line 667). Consider either removing it or merging its 3-level case into the existing breadcrumb test.

output.feedback: The change is correct and complete. Exactly the three unused named imports were removed: `parseHash` from `./router.js`, and `parentPath` + `basename` from `./format.js`. The remaining required imports are all intact and confirmed used in the module body: from `./router.js` — `getCurrentRoute` (doSearch, render), `subscribe` (startApp), `toBrowseHash` (doSearch, renderBreadcrumb, makeParentRow, makeBrowseRow), `toSearchHash` (doSearch), `navigate` (doSearch, makeNavLink), and the `Route` type (renderBrowse/renderSearch signatures); from `./format.js` — `formatBytes`, `normalizeRelativePath`, `joinPath`, `formatDate`. Implementation is complete, correct, and well-tested. `makeActionButton` wraps the handler so both synchronous throws (outer try/catch — necessary because `handler()` is evaluated as an argument to `Promise.resolve` before the promise is created, so a bare `.catch` would miss a sync throw) and async rejections (`.catch`) surface errors to the user via the `.status` footer. The error format exactly matches `render()`'s catch block, giving a single consistent error surface as intended. The success path is provably unchanged: tests assert no 'Error' text appears on success or cancel, and the handler still calls `render()` on success. A clear docstring documents the wrapping contract. Comprehensive new tests cover rejecting Delete/Move/Copy (500/400/409), a non-Error bare-string rejection (exercising the `String(err)` fallback), the results-table-left-intact invariant, unchanged success paths, and cancel paths. All 250 tests pass. Only `makeActionButton` was touched — scope is correctly contained. One minor DRY observation: the error-formatting expression is now duplicated in three places (render() catch + the two branches of makeActionButton's wrapper); extracting a tiny `showError(err)` helper would consolidate it, but this is non-blocking since the task explicitly recommended the inline form.

The handleUpload implementation satisfies every requirement in the task spec. Each per-file api.upload call is wrapped in its own try/catch inside a sequential for...of loop, preserving order and preventing one failure from aborting the rest. Failed file names are collected in a local `failed: string[]`. After the loop, uploadInput.value is always cleared, render() is awaited to refresh the listing, and failures are surfaced with the exact requested message format `Uploaded N file(s); failed: a.txt, b.txt` (where N = files.length - failed.length). The full-success path is correctly left alone via the `if (failed.length > 0)` guard, so the re-render's normal status summary persists. The upload endpoint, input clearing, and re-render on success are unchanged. Comments clearly explain the deliberate ordering (render before surfacing failure so the message isn't overwritten). The docstring was updated to reflect the new behavior. Tests: the original 'uploads each selected file' and 'uploads nothing' tests still pass, plus four new per-file-error tests (middle-fails, first-fails, all-fail, all-succeed) verify ordering, input clearing, re-render, and failure surfacing — 245/245 pass. No dead code, no scope expansion, follows existing patterns.

Both lifecycle bugs are fixed exactly as specified. (a) The unsubscribe teardown is stored module-level, cleared-and-recalled on every startApp re-mount, guaranteeing at most one active hashchange listener per mount. (b) A monotonic renderToken is captured per render() invocation, and a staleness check after every await (browse fetch, search fetch, and in the catch block) bails out before touching resultsEl/breadcrumbEl/statusEl, so a slower superseded fetch can never clobber fresh results. The changes are minimally scoped, follow the existing comment/variable-placement conventions, and are well documented. The outdated afterEach comment in app.test.ts was correctly updated, and three new lifecycle tests (listener cleanup adds/removes, race discard for results, race discard for breadcrumb/status, plus a non-race control case) pin the new contract. Verification: `npx tsc --noEmit` passes with no errors and `npm test` passes all 246 tests. No issues found.

The test suite in FileServiceTests.cs has been correctly updated for the lazy-init refactoring. All three constructor tests that originally asserted Directory.Exists immediately after construction (Constructor_CreatesRootDirectory_WhenItDoesNotExist, Constructor_SupportsNestedHomeDirectoryPath, Constructor_NullHomeDirectory_DefaultsToHome) now properly call service.ResolveFullPath("") to trigger lazy initialization before asserting directory existence. Three new dedicated lazy-init tests have been added: LazyInit_CreatesRootDirectory_OnFirstMethodCall (Theory testing Resolve/Browse/Search entry points), LazyInit_IsSafe_UnderConcurrentFirstAccess (thread-safety under 32 concurrent threads), and Constructor_NormalizesHomeDirectory_ToFullPath. All 43 FileServiceTests pass, all 203 total tests pass, and the solution builds cleanly. The tests are independent, well-structured, use unique temp directories for isolation, and cover edge cases including path traversal attacks, concurrent access, search result caps, and recursive operations. The test descriptions clearly explain the lazy-init behavior. No tautological, dead, or disabled tests were found.

The test additions are well-written, comprehensive, and follow existing project patterns perfectly. The normalization tests (lines ~273-421) are particularly strong:

**Strengths:**
1. **Focused tests**: Each test group targets exactly one normalization rule (backslashes, leading/trailing slashes, repeated slashes, dot segments)

6. **Consistent patterns**: Uses the same FakeFileService and assertion patterns as existing tests

**No issues found:**
- No tautological or useless tests
- No dead or disabled tests
- All assertions are meaningful and test distinct behaviors
- Tests are independent and don't rely on execution order

The lazy initialization refactor is complete and correct. (1) The field is now `private readonly Lazy<string> _rootLazy;`. (2) The constructor body no longer calls `Directory.CreateDirectory`; it only constructs the Lazy, with `LazyThreadSafetyMode.ExecutionAndPublication` ensuring exactly-once creation under concurrent first-access. (3) The Lazy's value factory both creates the directory and returns the resolved root path, matching the spec. (4) The `Root` property (`=> _rootLazy.Value`) exposes the materialized root. (5) All former reads of `_root` in `SafeResolve` and `ToRelative` now go through `Root`; a grep for `_root ` confirms zero remaining references. (6) The public surface is unchanged — constructor signature `(IOptions<FileServiceOptions>, IWebHostEnvironment)` and all `IFileService` method signatures are intact, so DI registration and callers are unaffected. (7) Documentation is updated coherently: the class XML doc describes the lazy creation, and the in-ctor comment explains the deferral and thread-safety rationale. (8) `dotnet build TestProject.sln` succeeds with 0 warnings and 0 errors. The behavior change (directory not created until first method call) is the intended and documented one; the three affected constructor tests are explicitly deferred to the test-writing phase per the task description, so they are not flagged here. No quality issues found.

The implementation correctly adds a `NormalizeRelativePath` private static helper that mirrors the frontend's `normalizeRelativePath` rules (replace backslashes, split on `/`, filter empty/`.`/`..` segments, join with `/`, empty for null/empty). It is applied only to the response path while the `_service.UploadAsync` call continues to receive the raw query value, so upload success/failure status codes and the service interaction are unchanged. Behavior is identical for already-canonical inputs. The helper is documented with an XML doc comment and integrates cleanly with the existing controller style (`System.Linq` is available via ImplicitUsings). Comprehensive tests cover backslashes, leading/trailing slashes, repeated slashes, dot/dotdot segments, collapse-to-empty, and mixed input, plus a regression test confirming the raw path is forwarded to the service. Full suite: 227 passed, 0 failed. No completion, compliance, dead-code, readability, or behavior-preservation issues found.

The deduplication has NOT been implemented. Both src/api.test.ts (line 12-26) and src/app.test.ts (line 51-64) still contain their own inline copy of the mockResponse helper function. No shared module (src/test-utils/mock-response.ts, src/__test-utils__/mock-response.ts, or equivalent) exists anywhere in the repository. The two inline copies are functionally different in their json() fallback: api.test.ts parses the text-only input as JSON (opts.body ?? (opts.text ? JSON.parse(opts.text) : {})), while app.test.ts returns an empty object (opts.body ?? {}). The characterization tests at the bottom of api.test.ts already encode the desired union semantics (including the text-only-as-JSON branch), which is excellent — they would validate the shared module after extraction. The tsconfig.json exclude array ('src/**/*.test.ts') would also need updating to prevent a non-test shared module from being included in the production build.

issues.description: The inline mockResponse function (lines 12-26) must be replaced with an import from the new shared module (e.g., import { mockResponse } from './test-utils/mock-response'). The existing characterization tests (the 'mockResponse helper' describe block) already test the union semantics and should continue to work against the extracted module without changes.

The inline mockResponse function (lines 51-64) must be replaced with an import from the new shared module. The app.test.ts copy uses a simpler json() fallback (returns {} when body is absent); the shared module should use the api.test.ts version (parses text as JSON when only text is provided) which is the union/superset behavior. All existing app.test.ts call sites always supply a body, so this change is backward-compatible.

The shared module (e.g., src/test-utils/mock-response.ts or src/__test-utils__/mock-response.ts) does not exist and must be created. It should export mockResponse with the union semantics from api.test.ts, and optionally export a MockResponseOptions type/interface.

The exclude array currently only excludes 'src/**/*.test.ts'. Any new non-test shared utility file (e.g., src/test-utils/mock-response.ts) would be included in the production tsc build. Add the appropriate directory to the exclude array (e.g., 'src/__test-utils__/**' or 'src/test-utils/**') to prevent shipping test utilities to wwwroot/dist.

The tests in src/app.test.ts are well-written and correctly pre-adapted for the route-parameter removal. All 272 tests pass (82 in app.test.ts). Key findings:

1. **Signature alignment**: All calls to renderBrowse() and renderSearch() already use the single-argument (result-only) form — no test still passes a second `route` argument. The tests are forward-compatible with the post-refactor signatures.

2. **No Route type import in tests**: The test file does not import `Route` from router, which is correct — it was never needed in the tests.

3. **Characterization adequacy**: The 'render output depends only on result (route is irrelevant)' describe block is exemplary for a refactor task. It pins the key invariant with snapshot-style tests that render the same result under two completely different active routes (browse→search and search→browse) and assert byte-for-byte identical output. This will catch any regression if a future change causes renderBrowse/renderSearch to accidentally depend on the active route.

4. **Edge cases covered**: Empty browse result (zero folders/files/size), empty search result (0 results for ...), breadcrumb path normalization (doubled/trailing slashes), search scope path in breadcrumb, and table-swap behavior (renderSearch after renderBrowse and vice versa).

5. **No issues found**: No tautological tests, no dead/disabled tests, no over-mocking, tests are independent and well-documented. The 82-test suite is comprehensive without being bloated.

The only remaining work is on the implementation side (src/app.ts): remove the `route: Route` parameter from both signatures, remove the two `void route;` lines, remove the now-unused `import type { Route }` (only referenced in those two signatures — line 31), and update render() to call `renderBrowse(result)` / `renderSearch(result)` instead of `(result, route)`.

output.feedback: Approved. The four dead CSS rule blocks were removed precisely as specified in src/app.css, and no other rules were modified (verified via git diff and grep). The dead-code diagnosis is correct: app.ts renders class-less '/' separators (no .separator) and sets the upload label to className='btn' (no .upload-btn), and no table row ever gets .folder-row. All 264 tests pass, including app.css.test.ts which asserts on the untouched .browser-dialog rules. The engineer also added regression-guard tests in app.test.ts that pin the dead-code contract; this is a reasonable, behavior-preserving enhancement that formalizes the task's own verification steps. One trivial cosmetic nit: the removals left a couple of extra blank lines in the CSS, but this does not alter any rules or behavior.

The Browse method refactor is correctly and cleanly implemented. The double-traversal pattern (Directory.GetDirectories + Directory.GetFiles, each materializing an array) has been replaced with a single `new DirectoryInfo(full).EnumerateFileSystemInfos()` enumeration, exactly as specified.

Verified requirements:
- Single-pass enumeration via EnumerateFileSystemInfos() replaces the two GetDirectories/GetFiles calls and their two array allocations. The explanatory comment is accurate and helpful.
- Correct type-branching: `if (info is DirectoryInfo directoryInfo)` for directories, else a direct `(FileInfo)info` cast for files. The cast in the else branch is guaranteed safe since EnumerateFileSystemInfos yields only DirectoryInfo or FileInfo instances.
- Output contract fully preserved per-entry: Name (info.Name), Path (ToRelative of FullName), IsDirectory, Size (0 for dirs, info.Length for files), LastModified (info.LastWriteTimeUtc).
- Sorting unchanged: entries.Sort(CompareEntries) preserves directories-before-files with OrdinalIgnoreCase name ordering within each group.
- Aggregations unchanged: folderCount/fileCount/totalSize computed in the same post-sort loop; parent = ComputeParent(relative) is computed identically.
- No other service methods were touched (Search still uses GetDirectories/GetFiles, which is correct per the task scope of Browse-only).
- The lazy `Root` accessor (`private string Root => _rootLazy.Value`) is used throughout, as required.
- Build produces 0 warnings, 0 errors.
- Test verification: all 221 tests pass, including all 32 Browse_* tests in FileServiceTests. The added characterization tests (empty directory, files-only, dirs-only, mixed-case sorting, zero-byte files, special characters in names, total-size summation, whitespace-path resolution, mixed dirs/files ordering) are valuable and correctly pin down the output contract.

No quality issues found.

The tests for FileService.Move are thorough and effectively validate the fix for the null-forgiving operator. They cover normal operations (file and directory moves), parent directory creation, edge cases like existing destinations and invalid paths, and specifically the null-parent scenario by misconfiguring the home directory as the filesystem root. The null-parent test correctly verifies that an ArgumentException (not ArgumentNullException) is thrown with an appropriate message, ensuring the fix prevents crashes from opaque errors.

The new tests in FileServiceTests.cs are well-written and correctly pin down the post-fix behavior for the TOCTOU race in Delete. The two new edge-case tests (Delete_NonExistentFile_SucceedsSilently and Delete_NonExistentNestedPath_SucceedsSilently) are precise, independent, use clear descriptions, and follow existing project patterns (xUnit, temp directories, CreateService helper). Delete_NonExistentNestedPath_SucceedsSilently is the key characterization test: it currently fails against the unfixed implementation (File.Delete throws DirectoryNotFoundException because the parent dir is missing), and will pass once the implementation is correctly fixed with a try/catch pattern that treats 'already gone' as success. The other new test (Delete_EmptyDirectory_Succeeds) is a valid edge case. All 14 existing Delete tests continue to pass. No tautological, dead, or disabled tests found. No issues with the tests themselves.

The fix is complete and correct. The null-forgiving operator (`!`) has been removed from `Path.GetDirectoryName(destination)`. The implementation now captures the result into `var parent = Path.GetDirectoryName(destination);`, checks `string.IsNullOrEmpty(parent)`, and throws a clear `ArgumentException("Invalid destination path", nameof(request))` before calling `Directory.CreateDirectory(parent)`. A well-written comment explains the rationale (filesystem-root destinations are nonsensical within the sandbox).

Scope compliance: Only the Move method's parent-directory handling changed. The Directory.Move / File.Move dispatch and all other Move behavior are untouched.

The task-16 lazy `Root` accessor is in use; no `_root` field references remain.

A new specification test `Move_DestinationParentIsFileSystemRoot_ThrowsArgumentExceptionNotArgumentNull` was added; it misconfigures the home directory to the filesystem root, moves to an empty destination (resolving to that root), and strictly asserts the thrown exception is a plain ArgumentException (not the ArgumentNullException that the unfixed code would produce). All 217 tests pass.

The test suite for the recursion depth limit feature is well-designed and comprehensive. Key strengths:

1. **Boundary coverage is precise**: Tests cover depth 31 (just below limit), depth 32 (exact limit), and depths 33/50 (beyond limit). This pins the exact boundary behavior.
2. **Happy-path tests verify completeness**: The within-limit theory test creates files at both the root and leaf of the chain and verifies both source integrity and full destination mirroring after copy.
3. **Error-path tests verify exact exception**: IOException with message 'Directory copy depth limit exceeded (possible cycle)' is asserted, matching the task specification.
4. **Source integrity test is valuable**: The 'LeavesSourceUntouched' fact test ensures that even when the depth limit is exceeded, the source directory tree is never mutated — this is an important safety invariant.
5. **Helper method is well-implemented**: `CreateNestedChain` is clean, reusable, and correctly builds the chain structure needed by all depth limit tests.
6. **Tests follow existing patterns**: Uses the same `CreateService()` setup, temp directory isolation, and xUnit conventions as the rest of the test file.
7. **All 4 new tests currently fail as expected** — the implementation has not yet added the `depth` parameter or `MaxCopyDepth` constant. These failing tests serve as a precise specification for the implementation step.
8. **All 215 existing tests pass**, confirming the depth limit tests don't regress anything.

No issues found with test quality, coverage, or structure.

Implementation fully satisfies the task. Verified against each requirement:

1. CopyDirectory signature changed to `CopyDirectory(string source, string destination, int depth)` ✓
2. Public `Copy(CopyRequest)` seeds the recursion at `depth: 0` ✓
3. Recursive call passes `depth + 1` ✓
4. `private const int MaxCopyDepth = 32;` defined with a thorough docstring ✓
5. Guard throws `new IOException("Directory copy depth limit exceeded (possible cycle)")` when `depth >= MaxCopyDepth` ✓
6. CopyDirectory docstring updated to describe the depth limit and its rationale ✓
7. Public `Copy(CopyRequest)` signature unchanged ✓
8. Lazy `Root` accessor from task-16 is used throughout (no stray `_root` field remains); directory materialization happens on first `Root` access ✓

The off-by-one semantics are correct: the guard trips when `depth == MaxCopyDepth` (i.e., 32 levels of recursion), so a tree with 31 nested subdirectories copies successfully and one with 32+ throws. This matches the test matrix (`[InlineData(31)]` succeeds, `[InlineData(32)]` throws).

The full suite passes (219 passed, 0 failed).

5. Guard throws `new IOException("Directory copy depth limit exceeded (possible cycle)")` when `depth >= MaxCopyDepth` ✓
6. CopyDirectory docstring updated to describe the depth limit and its rationale ✓
7. Public `Copy(CopyRequest)` signature unchanged ✓
8. Lazy `Root` accessor from task-16 is used throughout (no stray `_root` field remains); directory materialization happens on first `Root` access ✓

The off-by-one semantics are correct: the guard trips when `depth == MaxCopyDepth` (i.e., 32 levels of recursion), so a tree with 31 nested subdirectories copies successfully and one with 32+ throws. This matches the test matrix (`[InlineData(31)]` succeeds, `[InlineData(32)]` throws).

Test phase landed comprehensive coverage: `Copy_DirectoryChain_WithinDepthLimit_CopiesCompletely`, `Copy_DirectoryChain_AtOrBeyondDepthLimit_ThrowsIOException` (asserts exact IOException message), and `Copy_DeeplyNestedDirectory_BeyondLimit_LeavesSourceUntouched` to confirm the source is not modified on abort. The full suite passes (219 passed, 0 failed).

No dead code, no scope creep, no divergent patterns, no leftover debug statements. The `depth` parameter is documented via `<param name="depth">`. Code is clean and ready to merge.

The TOCTOU race in FileService.Delete is correctly fixed. The git diff shows exactly the intended change and nothing more: the check-then-act `if (Directory.Exists(path))` guard is removed and replaced with the recommended race-free pattern — attempt `Directory.Delete(path, recursive: true)`, fall through to `File.Delete(path)` on `DirectoryNotFoundException`, and treat a final 'already gone' `DirectoryNotFoundException` as success. The required docstring phrasing is present ('Deleting a non-existent path succeeds silently — the post-condition (entry gone) is already met') and thoroughly explains the rationale. SafeResolve still runs first and remains the sole path-escape gate (ArgumentException preserved). The task-16 lazy Root accessor is used; no legacy `_root` field remains (grep confirmed zero matches). Scope is correctly contained to Delete only — Move/Copy retain their existing Exists checks, which is appropriate given the task did not ask for them. Tests all pass (215 total, 15 Delete-related), including the new coverage for the empty-directory branch, non-existent file idempotency, and the nested-path case that directly reproduces the original race failure mode. No dead code, no unused artifacts, no leftover debug statements, and no scope expansion. Note: the inline comment's claim that `Directory.Delete` on a real file throws `DirectoryNotFoundException` is accurate on this runtime/platform (verified by passing tests) and matches the task's explicitly recommended approach; I am not flagging the inherent platform nuance as it is by design of the requested pattern rather than an implementer deviation.

The rewrite of FileService.ComputeParent in Services/FileService.cs is complete and correct. The manual `relative.LastIndexOf('/')` + `Substring(0, separatorIndex)` logic was replaced with `Path.GetDirectoryName(relative)?.Replace('\', '/') ?? string.Empty`, exactly as specified. The explicit `if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(relative)) return null;` guard is preserved so the divergent null-for-empty contract holds (verified: Path.GetDirectoryName returns null for empty on this target, but the guard guarantees it regardless of platform). Single-segment, multi-segment, dotted-segment, and spaced-segment inputs all produce identical outputs to the old implementation (confirmed via a standalone probe and via the test suite). The method remains pure `private static` with no access to the lazy `Root` accessor. The docstring accurately describes the three-valued contract (null / "" / parent). All 225 tests pass, including the three named verification tests (Browse_Subdirectory_ReturnsRelativePathAndEmptyParent, Browse_DeepSubdirectory_ParentIsRelativeParentPath, Browse_Root_ReturnsDirectoriesFirstThenFiles_SortedOrdinalIgnoreCase) and the four newly added characterization tests (Browse_ThreeSegmentPath_ParentIsTwoSegmentPath, Browse_FourSegmentPath_ParentIsThreeSegmentPath, Browse_ParentSegmentContainingDots_IsPreservedVerbatim, Browse_ParentSegmentContainingSpaces_IsPreservedVerbatim). No critical issues. One non-blocking observation: the task was narrowly scoped to rewriting ComputeParent, and the implementation also added four characterization tests to FileServiceTests.cs. These are a beneficial, well-commented scope addition that strengthens verification of the refactor rather than a defect — they do not alter production behavior — so I am accepting them. Note that after the null/empty guard, GetDirectoryName never returns null for the surviving inputs on this target, making `?? string.Empty` defensive-but-redundant; it is idiomatic under the project's nullable-enabled context and harmless, so no change is required.

The test suite is critically incomplete and will not adequately catch regressions during the refactoring. issues.description: Missing tests for the five specific exception types (ArgumentException, UnauthorizedAccessException, DirectoryNotFoundException, FileNotFoundException, IOException) being mapped to 400 responses in each endpoint. The refactoring consolidates this behavior into a helper, but current tests likely only check for one or two exceptions, not all five.

Missing edge case tests: empty/null parameters, path normalization in responses, content type handling in Download, and the 'non-swallowing' guarantee for unhandled exceptions. The refactoring must preserve these behaviors, but without dedicated tests, regressions are possible.

Missing test for the Upload endpoint's async behavior and the ExecuteAsync helper path. The refactoring introduces a new async helper, but there may be no test verifying that async exceptions are properly caught and mapped.

The test 'Controller_DoesNotSwallowUnhandledExceptions' is mentioned but may be insufficient. It must explicitly verify that exceptions NOT in the five listed (e.g., NullReferenceException) still propagate, and this behavior must be tested for each endpoint after refactoring.

output.feedback:
The structural refactor is complete, faithful, and behavior-preserving. All seven planned modules exist under src/app/ with exactly the exports the task specified: dom-builders.ts (buildTable, makeNavLink, makeParentRow, makeBrowseRow, makeActionButton, makeSearchRow, renderBreadcrumb), render-browse.ts (renderBrowse), render-search.ts (renderSearch), toolbar-handlers.ts (doSearch, handleUpload), render-orchestrator.ts (render + init(refs) owning renderToken/unsubscribe), and context.ts holding the shared refs/getters/setters (an option the task explicitly permitted). app.ts is now a slim entry that exports startApp, re-exports renderBrowse/renderSearch, and keeps the bootstrap guard — so src/app.test.ts's existing `import { startApp, renderBrowse, renderSearch } from './app'` resolves unmodified.

Behavior preservation verified by line-by-line comparison against git HEAD:src/app.ts. Every helper maps 1:1 with only the indirection swaps the context pattern requires (resultsEl→getResults(), api→getApi(), statusEl→getStatus(), render()→rerender()). The render-hook indirection is sound: init() calls setRenderHook(render) BEFORE the initial render(), so action-button handlers (created only during render) always invoke the real render, and the default no-op hook is never reachable from a real click. The render-race token and listener-teardown logic moved intact into render-orchestrator.ts, preserving the task-20 fixes. The chosen context+hook pattern cleanly breaks the would-be cycle (orchestrator→render-browse→dom-builders→orchestrator) and is well documented in context.ts — a better solution than the 'lazy import' the task mentioned as an alternative.

Verification results: `npx tsc --noEmit` exits 0 (clean type-check). `npm test` passes all 301 tests, including the 82 unmodified app.test.ts scenarios and 40 new characterization tests (src/app.characterization.test.ts) that pin exact DOM/attribute/lifecycle details. No unused imports/variables, no dead code, no debug statements, no leftover TODOs, and all new public functions carry docstrings. Code follows existing conventions (textContent/element creation for user strings, setAttribute for hrefs, .js import specifiers).

One environment note (NOT a code defect): `npm run build` fails at the copy-assets.mjs step because node_modules/bootstrap-icons/font/bootstrap-icons.css is absent in this worktree — a stale/incomplete node_modules state unrelated to the refactor. The TypeScript compilation half (`tsc`) succeeds; the failure is on a static-asset copy that has no connection to these changes and would fail identically on the base branch. No action needed in code.

CORE RELOCATION IS CORRECT AND VERIFIED. The four in-scope changes all landed exactly as specified: (1) new Configuration/FileServiceOptions.cs declares `namespace TestProject.Configuration;` with the unchanged class body; (2) the old root FileServiceOptions.cs is deleted (git status: `D FileServiceOptions.cs`); (3) Program.cs now uses the fully-qualified `TestProject.Configuration.FileServiceOptions` in the Configure<T>(...) call (one of the two options the task permitted); (4) Services/FileService.cs gained `using TestProject.Configuration;`. A fifth necessary edit — adding `using TestProject.Configuration;` to TestProject.Tests/Services/FileServiceTests.cs — is justified: that file directly constructs `new FileServiceOptions` (lines 56, 117, 852), so without it the test project would not compile. No behavior change was introduced by the move (class body is byte-identical aside from the namespace), satisfying the refactor-preservation requirement.

output.feedback: The implementation is complete, correct, and well-documented. Both Execute (sync) and ExecuteAsync (async) helpers were extracted with the identical 5-catch translation list, and all seven endpoints (Browse, Search, Upload, Download, Delete, Move, Copy) delegate to them as one-liners while preserving their XML doc comments. The build succeeds with 0 warnings/errors and all 287 tests pass without modification, including the per-endpoint unhandled-exception-propagation tests, subtype-exception cases (ArgumentNullException, ArgumentOutOfRangeException, PathTooLongException), exact-payload-shape assertions, and single-invocation-counting tests. Observable behavior is fully preserved: status codes (200/400), payload shapes (error/success/path), the exception-to-BadRequest mapping, and the 'non-translated exceptions propagate' guarantee. The prior review concern about test-suite gaps has been comprehensively addressed in FilesControllerTests.cs. One justified deviation: the helper return type is ActionResult rather than the task's literal IActionResult — this is necessary because Browse/Search return ActionResult<T>, and IActionResult does not implicitly convert to ActionResult<T> while ActionResult does (and implements IActionResult). The implementer documented this reasoning thoroughly in a <remarks> block. This is the minimal correct type for a unified helper and behavior is identical. The remaining sync/async duplication is inherent to C# and is exactly what the task specified (two helpers), so it is not a DRY violation worth flagging. No quality issues found.

The split is a clean, behavior-preserving refactor. VERIFIED COMPLETION: (1) All 89 `it()` test scenarios are preserved exactly — I extracted every test title from the original monolith and from the 5 split files and confirmed a perfect multiset match (0 dropped, 0 added, 0 renamed). I also diffed the full body of every top-level describe block (whitespace-normalized); the ONLY textual difference in any assertion/comment is `'./app'` → `'../app'` in one comment, which is the correct path adjustment for the new `src/app/` location. (2) `npm test` passes: 333 tests across 12 files, including 89 in the 5 new split files. For comparison, I stashed the changes and re-ran the ORIGINAL monolith — it had 5 FAILURES (`ReferenceError: browseRoute is not defined` / `searchRoute is not defined` in the dead-CSS characterization suite). Those now pass because `browseRoute`/`searchRoute` are defined in `test-helpers.ts`, which the task EXPLICITLY requires to be extracted ('browseRoute, searchRoute ... must be extracted into a shared helper module'). This is the minimal, in-scope way to satisfy both 'PRESERVE EVERY EXISTING TEST SCENARIO' and 'npm test passes'. (3) `npx tsc --noEmit` exits 0; `test-helpers.ts` is correctly excluded from the production build (verified: `wwwroot/dist/app/` contains no test-helpers.js and no .test.js files). DESCRIBE-BLOCK PLACEMENT: the task's per-file breakdown names 5 describe blocks, but the monolith actually contains 10 top-level blocks. The implementer correctly preserved ALL of them (dropping any would be a CRITICAL violation) by placing the extra blocks in the most thematically-related files: 'import characterization' + 'dead CSS selector characterization' → start-app.test.ts (both pin app.ts's module surface/retained imports/DOM output); 'render output depends only on result' + 'rendering lifecycle' → render-orchestration.test.ts (render coordination/lifecycle). Each file's JSDoc header documents its full contents and the grouping rationale. HELPER EXTRACTION: `src/app/test-helpers.ts` exports every required name (fileEntry, browseResult, joinPathHelper, browseRoute, searchRoute, flush, SetupCtx, setup, setupCleared, dataRows, rowByName, cellsOf, clickNameLink, buttonsByText, fetchMock, installAppTestLifecycle). `mockResponse` is correctly IMPORTED from the existing `src/test-utils/mock-response.ts` (task-27) rather than re-defined. tsconfig.json adds `src/app/test-helpers.ts` to `exclude`, mirroring the `src/test-utils/**` pattern. CLEANLINESS: I checked every import in all 5 files — zero unused imports (including the multi-name single-line vitest/router/format imports). No dead code, no debug leftovers, no duplicated logic, no magic literals (ISO/ISO_FMT are named constants). `installAppTestLifecycle()` is called exactly once per file (vitest scopes hooks per-file, so this is correct). The `export let fetchMock` live-binding mechanism (rebound in beforeEach) is sound and documented; all fetchMock.mock.calls assertions pass. The original `src/app.test.ts` is deleted. ORGANIZATION: each file has a single responsibility, a thorough JSDoc header carrying over the monolith's contract decisions, and consistent section dividers matching the original style. No issues found.

output.summary: Reviewed the full file-browser changeset (ASP.NET `FilesController`/`FileService`, `Program.cs`, and the TypeScript SPA). Path handling for the `path`/`sourcePath`/`destinationPath` parameters is correctly sandboxed via `SafeResolve` (uses `Path.GetFullPath` + root-prefix containment check), the SPA consistently uses `textContent`/`encodeURIComponent` (no XSS), search is result-bounded, and `CopyDirectory` has a recursion-depth guard. However, the `Upload` endpoint feeds the fully attacker-controlled multipart `file.FileName` straight into `Path.Combine(dir, file.FileName)` + `File.Create` with NO sanitization or containment re-check, yielding an unauthenticated arbitrary-file-write / path-traversal vulnerability (critical). Secondary issues: every file-management endpoint is completely unauthenticated (amplifies the write bug; also allows anyone to read/delete all home files), and raw .NET exception messages — which include absolute server filesystem paths — are echoed back to clients in 400 responses.

findings.title: Arbitrary file write / path traversal via unsanitized uploaded file name
findings.fixPrompt: In `Services/FileService.cs`, harden `UploadAsync(string relativeDirPath, IFormFile file)` so the client-supplied filename cannot escape the home root. After resolving and creating the directory: (1) derive a safe leaf name with `var leafName = Path.GetFileName(file.FileName);` (this strips any leading directories, `..` segments, drive roots, and absolute paths); (2) reject empty or invalid names — `if (string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(leafName) || leafName.IndexOfAny(Path.GetInvalidFileNameChars()) >= 0) throw new ArgumentException("Invalid file name");`; (3) compute `var path = Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine(dir, leafName));` and re-apply the SAME containment check used by `SafeResolve` (i.e., `path == Root` or `path.StartsWith(Root + Path.DirectorySeparatorChar, StringComparison.Ordinal)`), throwing `ArgumentException("Invalid file name")` if it fails; (4) only then `await using var fs = File.Create(path); await file.CopyToAsync(fs);`. Optionally expose a small private helper (e.g. `ResolveChildPath(dir, leafName)`) so the containment logic isn't duplicated. Then, in `Controllers/FilesController.cs`, change the `Upload` response path construction to use the same sanitized leaf: replace `file.FileName` with `System.IO.Path.GetFileName(file.FileName ?? string.Empty)` in both branches of `var resultPath = string.IsNullOrEmpty(dir) ? ... : $"{dir}/{...}";`. Finally add regression tests in `TestProject.Tests/Services/FileServiceTests.cs` that assert `UploadAsync("", FormFileFactory.CreateFormFile("../../escape.txt", "x"))` and an absolute/rooted filename both throw `ArgumentException` and that no file is created outside the home root (e.g., assert `File.Exists(Path.Combine(root, "..", "escape.txt"))` is false).

findings.title: File-management API exposes browse/read/write/delete with no authentication or authorization
findings.fixPrompt: Add an explicit, documented access-control decision for the `/api/files/*` surface in `Controllers/FilesController.cs` and `Program.cs`. Preferred: introduce ASP.NET Core authentication/authorization (e.g., cookie or JWT) — call `builder.Services.AddAuthentication(...).Add...(...)` and `builder.Services.AddAuthorization();`, then `app.UseAuthentication(); app.UseAuthorization();` in the pipeline before `MapControllers()`, and decorate the controller (or each mutating action) with `[Authorize]`. If authentication is intentionally out of scope for this sample app, add a startup guard that refuses to serve the API unless explicitly enabled for local-only use — e.g., when not in Development, require `builder.Configuration["FileService:AllowRemoteAccess"]` to be `"true"` else throw at startup, and/or restrict the endpoints to localhost via a middleware/`UseCors` allow-list of origins. Document the chosen posture in the controller XML doc comment and `README.md` so the open-by-default behavior is not accidentally deployed.

findings.title: Raw .NET exception messages (including absolute server paths) echoed to clients
findings.fixPrompt: In `Controllers/FilesController.cs`, stop returning raw `ex.Message` to clients. Replace each `BadRequest(new { error = ex.Message })` in both `Execute` and `ExecuteAsync` with a generic, mapped client-facing string and log the full exception server-side. Introduce a small private helper, e.g. `private static string ClientMessage(Exception ex) => ex switch { ArgumentException => "Invalid request", UnauthorizedAccessException => "Access denied", DirectoryNotFoundException or FileNotFoundException => "Not found", IOException => "I/O error", _ => "Request failed" };` and use `BadRequest(new { error = ClientMessage(ex) })`. If an `ILogger<FilesController>` is available (inject it via the constructor), call `_logger.LogError(ex, "Files API failure")` inside each catch before returning so the real message is preserved in server logs only. Do not change which exception types map to 400, and keep the existing test expectations for status code 400; update `FilesControllerTests.cs`'s `CaughtExceptions` assertions to expect the generic message instead of `ex.Message`.

output.summary: I reviewed every runtime change in the changeset: the C# backend (Services/FileService.cs, Controllers/FilesController.cs, Program.cs, Configuration/FileServiceOptions.cs) and the refactored TypeScript frontend (src/app.ts plus the new src/app/context.ts, dom-builders.ts, render-browse.ts, render-search.ts, render-orchestrator.ts, toolbar-handlers.ts). Most of the changes are efficiency-positive or neutral: Browse now does a single-pass EnumerateFileSystemInfos instead of two GetDirectories/GetFiles traversals; the FileExtensionContentTypeProvider is now a static singleton instead of allocated per Download request; the lazy root defers one-time I/O off DI resolution; Delete dropped its redundant pre-check (Directory.Exists) for directories; and render-orchestrator.init tears down prior hashchange listeners (plugging a leak) and guards against stale commits via a render-race token. I found one genuine, low-severity missed optimization: the new render dispatcher cancels the *effect* of a superseded fetch (via the token) but still lets the fetch itself run to completion and discard the response, so rapid navigation issues concurrent requests whose results are thrown away.

findings.title: Superseded renders still run their fetch to completion (wasted client/server I/O)
findings.description: The newly-extracted `render()` in render-orchestrator.ts protects against stale commits with a monotonic `renderToken`: after each `await` it bails out if a newer render started, so a slower fetch cannot overwrite fresher results. However, the fetch itself is never cancelled. When the user navigates quickly (e.g. clicks into folder A, then folder B before A's browse resolves), both `getApi().browse(...)` / `getApi().search(...)` calls run concurrently to completion and the older response is simply discarded after the `if (myToken !== renderToken) return;` check. This is wasted work on both client and server (the request was fully serviced only to be thrown away), and is exactly the 'computing results that are then discarded / unnecessary I/O' pattern. Because the file-browser responses are small JSON and rapid navigation is occasional, the impact is minor — hence low severity — but the standard fix (AbortController) is a natural companion to the race token the author already added. Note: the prior monolithic render in app.ts had no guard at all, so this is an improvement with a remaining gap, not a regression.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/render-orchestrator.ts and src/api.ts, cancel superseded in-flight fetches instead of only discarding their results. Steps: (1) In src/api.ts, extend ApiClient.request to accept an optional `signal: AbortSignal` via the `init`/options and forward it to `fetch(url, init)`; thread an optional `AbortSignal` parameter through `browse()` and `search()` (and the other methods if convenient, though browse/search are the render-path ones). (2) In render-orchestrator.ts, add a module-level `let currentController: AbortController | null = null;` alongside `renderToken`. At the start of `render()`, create a new `AbortController`, assign it to `currentController`, and pass its `signal` into `getApi().browse(...)` / `getApi().search(...)`. Before issuing the new fetch (or right after creating the new controller), call `currentController?.abort()` is NOT what you want for the current one — instead, on each new render, abort the *previous* controller (capture the previous one before replacing it). (3) In the `catch` block of `render()`, treat an aborted request as a non-error: if the error's `name === 'AbortError'` (or the signal was aborted), return without writing error text to the results element — keep the existing `myToken !== renderToken` early-return as a second line of defense. Verify with the existing render-orchestration tests that rapid re-navigation still renders the freshest route and does not surface an 'Error:' status from the aborted request.

(2) In render-orchestrator.ts, add a module-level `let currentController: AbortController | null = null;` alongside `renderToken`. At the start of `render()`, create a new `AbortController`, assign it to `currentController`, and pass its `signal` into `getApi().browse(...)` / `getApi().search(...)`. Before issuing the new fetch (or right after creating the new controller), call `currentController?.abort()` is NOT what you want for the current one — instead, on each new render, abort the *previous* controller (capture the previous one before replacing it). (3) In the `catch` block of `render()`, treat an aborted request as a non-error: if the error's `name === 'AbortError'` (or the signal was aborted), return without writing error text to the results element — keep the existing `myToken !== renderToken` early-return as a second line of defense. Verify with the existing render-orchestration tests that rapid re-navigation still renders the freshest route and does not surface an 'Error:' status from the aborted request.

output.summary: I reviewed every runtime change in the changeset: the C# backend (Services/FileService.cs, Controllers/FilesController.cs, Program.cs, Configuration/FileServiceOptions.cs) and the refactored TypeScript frontend (src/app.ts plus the new src/app/context.ts, dom-builders.ts, render-browse.ts, render-search.ts, render-orchestrator.ts, toolbar-handlers.ts). Most of the changes are efficiency-positive or neutral: Browse now does a single-pass EnumerateFileSystemInfos instead of two GetDirectories/GetFiles traversals; the FileExtensionContentTypeProvider is now a static singleton instead of allocated per Download request; the lazy root defers one-time I/O off DI resolution; Delete dropped its redundant pre-check (Directory.Exists) for directories; and render-orchestrator.init tears down prior hashchange listeners (plugging a leak) and guards against stale commits via a render-race token. I found one genuine, low-severity missed optimization: the new render dispatcher cancels the *effect* of a superseded fetch (via the token) but still lets the fetch itself run to completion and discard the response, so rapid navigation issues concurrent requests whose results are thrown away.

findings.title: Superseded renders still run their fetch to completion (wasted client/server I/O)
findings.description: The newly-extracted `render()` in render-orchestrator.ts protects against stale commits with a monotonic `renderToken`: after each `await` it bails out if a newer render started, so a slower fetch cannot overwrite fresher results. However, the fetch itself is never cancelled. When the user navigates quickly (e.g. clicks into folder A, then folder B before A's browse resolves), both `getApi().browse(...)` / `getApi().search(...)` calls run concurrently to completion and the older response is simply discarded after the `if (myToken !== renderToken) return;` check. This is wasted work on both client and server (the request was fully serviced only to be thrown away), and is exactly the 'computing results that are then discarded / unnecessary I/O' pattern. Because the file-browser responses are small JSON and rapid navigation is occasional, the impact is minor — hence low severity — but the standard fix (AbortController) is a natural companion to the race token the author already added. Note: the prior monolithic render in app.ts had no guard at all, so this is an improvement with a remaining gap, not a regression.

output.summary: This workflow restructured the file-browser SPA (split src/app.ts into focused src/app/* modules: render-orchestrator, render-browse, render-search, dom-builders, toolbar-handlers, context) and hardened behavior — action-button errors now surface in the status footer, uploads report per-file partial failures, and a render-race guard was added. These are genuine UX improvements and the rendered DOM structure is identical to before (the app.css deletions only removed already-dead selectors). Reviewing the current UI surface, I found several real accessibility and interaction-feedback gaps in code within the changeset: the Upload control is unreachable for keyboard users, the search input lacks an accessible name, there is no loading indicator during async fetches, no empty state, and the secondary 'Browse Files' trigger has ambiguous microcopy. No critical breakage; ordered by severity below.

findings.title: Upload control is unreachable via keyboard (input is hidden via display:none)
findings.description: The file input is created with `uploadInput.hidden = true`, which applies the HTML `hidden` attribute → UA `display:none`. A `display:none` form control is removed from the tab order and cannot receive focus. The visible 'Upload' affordance is a wrapping `<label class="btn">`; labels themselves are not focusable/activatable via keyboard. Net effect: keyboard-only and screen-reader users have no way to focus or trigger the Upload control, while mouse users can (clicking the label forwards a click to the hidden input). This is a genuine WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard) failure for the upload flow. The pattern was preserved through the refactor and lives in code touched by this changeset.
findings.fixPrompt: In `src/app.ts`, the upload input is built around the line `uploadInput.hidden = true;` inside `startApp` (the `const uploadInput = document.createElement('input'); uploadInput.type = 'file'; uploadInput.hidden = true; uploadInput.multiple = true;` block). Remove `uploadInput.hidden = true;` (do not set the `hidden` attribute). Instead, keep the input focusable but visually hidden: give it a class such as `visually-hidden` (e.g. `uploadInput.className = 'visually-hidden';`) and add `uploadInput.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Upload files');`. Then in `src/app.css` add a `.visually-hidden { position: absolute; width: 1px; height: 1px; padding: 0; margin: -1px; overflow: hidden; clip: rect(0, 0, 0, 0); white-space: nowrap; border: 0; }` rule (a standard sr-only pattern that preserves focusability). Because the `<label class="btn">` still wraps the input, Tab will now move focus onto the (visually-hidden) input inside the visible 'Upload' button, and Enter/Space will open the file picker. Verify that a visible focus ring appears around the Upload button when focused (add a `:focus-within` style on `.toolbar label.btn` if the default outline does not show). Do not change any other behavior.

findings.title: Search input relies only on a placeholder, no accessible name
findings.description: The search input is created with `searchInput.type = 'text'` and `searchInput.placeholder = 'Search...'` but no associated `<label>`, `aria-label`, or `title`. A placeholder is not an accessible name: it is not exposed reliably to assistive technology and disappears as soon as the user types, leaving the field unlabeled. Screen-reader users get no clear purpose for the field. The field construction is part of the modified `app.ts`.
findings.fixPrompt: In `src/app.ts`, in the `startApp` toolbar-building block where `const searchInput = document.createElement('input'); searchInput.type = 'text'; searchInput.placeholder = 'Search...';` is defined, add an accessible name immediately after, e.g. `searchInput.setAttribute('aria-label', 'Search files');`. Keep the existing `placeholder` as a hint. Alternatively, add a visible `<label>` element and associate it via `for`/`id`. Make no other changes; ensure existing tests (`src/app/render-search.test.ts`, `src/app/toolbar-handlers.test.ts`) still query the input by its current selectors.

findings.title: No loading indicator while results are being fetched
findings.description: In `render()`, the results container is cleared synchronously (`resultsEl.innerHTML = '';`) and then the route data is awaited. During that await there is no loading, skeleton, or 'Loading…' state — the results panel is simply blank. The status footer is intentionally left untouched (to preserve navigation targets), so on the first load the user sees an empty table and empty status with no indication that work is in progress. This hurts perceived performance and can look like a broken/empty UI, especially on slower connections or larger directories. This lifecycle is newly authored in `render-orchestrator.ts`.
findings.fixPrompt: In `src/app/render-orchestrator.ts`, inside the `render()` function, immediately after the line `resultsEl.innerHTML = '';` (and before the `try`/`await getApi().browse(...)` / `search(...)` calls), insert a synchronous loading placeholder into the results element, e.g. `resultsEl.textContent = 'Loading…';`. This text is naturally replaced on success by `renderBrowse(result)` / `renderSearch(result)` (which set `resultsEl.innerHTML = ''` then append the table) and on error by the existing `resultsEl.textContent = 'Error: ' + ...` branch, so no additional cleanup is required. Keep the existing race-guard token logic and the intentional non-clearing of the breadcrumb/status intact.

findings.title: Empty folders render a bare table with no empty-state message
findings.description: `renderBrowse()` builds the results table and appends rows; if `result.entries` is empty (and there is no parent '..' row), the table shows only the column headers with an empty body and no explanation. Users perceive a blank/missing listing rather than an explicitly empty folder. `render-browse.ts` is newly authored in this workflow.
findings.fixPrompt: In `src/app/render-browse.ts`, in `renderBrowse()`, after the loop that appends rows (`for (const entry of result.entries) { tbody.append(makeBrowseRow(entry)); }`) and before `resultsEl.append(table);`, add a check: if `tbody.children.length === 0` (no parent row and no entries), append an empty-state row spanning all columns, e.g. create a `<tr>` with a single `<td colspan="4">` whose `textContent` is 'This folder is empty.' and append it to `tbody`. Use `document.createElement` / `textContent` only (no innerHTML). Do not alter the status footer or breadcrumb logic.

findings.title: 'Browse Files' trigger is ambiguous next to an already-visible browser
findings.description: The page renders a fully interactive embedded file browser as the primary surface, and directly below it a button labeled 'Browse Files'. Since files are already browsable inline, the button's purpose is unclear — a user may not realize it opens a second, dialog/modal view of the same browser. Consider clearer microcopy that communicates it opens a pop-out/dialog version.
findings.fixPrompt: In `src/app.ts`, locate the trigger button construction (`const trigger = document.createElement('button'); trigger.className = 'trigger'; trigger.textContent = 'Browse Files';`) and change `trigger.textContent` to a label that conveys it opens the dialog/pop-out view, e.g. 'Open in dialog' or 'Pop out browser'. Keep the class name and click handler unchanged. If a tooltip is desired, you may add `trigger.title = 'Open the file browser in a dialog window';`. No other behavior should change.

Make no other changes; ensure existing tests (`src/app/render-search.test.ts`, `src/app/toolbar-handlers.test.ts`) still query the input by its current selectors.

findings.title: No loading indicator while results are being fetched
findings.description: In `render()`, the results container is cleared synchronously (`resultsEl.innerHTML = '';`) and then the route data is awaited. During that await there is no loading, skeleton, or 'Loading…' state — the results panel is simply blank. The status footer is intentionally left untouched (to preserve navigation targets), so on the first load the user sees an empty table and empty status with no indication that work is in progress. This hurts perceived performance and can look like a broken/empty UI, especially on slower connections or larger directories. This lifecycle is newly authored in `render-orchestrator.ts`.
findings.fixPrompt: In `src/app/render-orchestrator.ts`, inside the `render()` function, immediately after the line `resultsEl.innerHTML = '';` (and before the `try`/`await getApi().browse(...)` / `search(...)` calls), insert a synchronous loading placeholder into the results element, e.g. `resultsEl.textContent = 'Loading…';`. This text is naturally replaced on success by `renderBrowse(result)` / `renderSearch(result)` (which set `resultsEl.innerHTML = ''` then append the table) and on error by the existing `resultsEl.textContent = 'Error: ' + ...` branch, so no additional cleanup is required. Keep the existing race-guard token logic and the intentional non-clearing of the breadcrumb/status intact.

findings.title: Empty folders render a bare table with no empty-state message
findings.description: `renderBrowse()` builds the results table and appends rows; if `result.entries` is empty (and there is no parent '..' row), the table shows only the column headers with an empty body and no explanation. Users perceive a blank/missing listing rather than an explicitly empty folder. `render-browse.ts` is newly authored in this workflow.
findings.fixPrompt: In `src/app/render-browse.ts`, in `renderBrowse()`, after the loop that appends rows (`for (const entry of result.entries) { tbody.append(makeBrowseRow(entry)); }`) and before `resultsEl.append(table);`, add a check: if `tbody.children.length === 0` (no parent row and no entries), append an empty-state row spanning all columns, e.g. create a `<tr>` with a single `<td colspan="4">` whose `textContent` is 'This folder is empty.' and append it to `tbody`. Use `document.createElement` / `textContent` only (no innerHTML). Do not alter the status footer or breadcrumb logic.

findings.title: 'Browse Files' trigger is ambiguous next to an already-visible browser
findings.description: The page renders a fully interactive embedded file browser as the primary surface, and directly below it a button labeled 'Browse Files'. Since files are already browsable inline, the button's purpose is unclear — a user may not realize it opens a second, dialog/modal view of the same browser. Consider clearer microcopy that communicates it opens a pop-out/dialog version.
findings.fixPrompt: In `src/app.ts`, locate the trigger button construction (`const trigger = document.createElement('button'); trigger.className = 'trigger'; trigger.textContent = 'Browse Files';`) and change `trigger.textContent` to a label that conveys it opens the dialog/pop-out view, e.g. 'Open in dialog' or 'Pop out browser'. Keep the class name and click handler unchanged. If a tooltip is desired, you may add `trigger.title = 'Open the file browser in a dialog window';`. No other behavior should change.

output.summary: Reviewed README.md, dev-diary.md, task.md, all XML doc comments on the changed C# surface (FilesController, FileService, IFileService, FileServiceOptions, Models), and JSDoc on the changed/added TypeScript modules (app.ts and the new src/app/* modules, format.ts, router.ts, api.ts, test utilities). Inline API documentation is thorough and matches the implementations; no docstring/code mismatches were found there. The findings are concentrated in README.md and dev-diary.md: the refactor changed two behaviors that the README still describes the old way (home-directory creation is now lazy, not at startup; a new 32-level copy-depth limit was introduced), and two pre-existing completeness gaps (undocumented search-result cap, and the browse response shape omitting two fields) are worth closing alongside them.

findings.title: README claims home folder is created "on startup" but refactor made creation lazy
findings.description: The Configuration section states: "The folder is created automatically on startup." This was true before this branch (the old `FileService` constructor called `Directory.CreateDirectory(root)` eagerly), but the refactor in `Services/FileService.cs` now wraps directory creation in a `Lazy<string>` (`_rootLazy`) that is only materialized on first access of the `Root` property — i.e. on the first file-system operation, not at startup/DI resolution. The class XML doc on `FileService` itself was correctly updated ("the directory itself is created lazily on first use"), but the README was not, so a user reading the docs is misled about when (and how late) misconfiguration or disk errors surface. The fix is to update the README sentence to describe lazy creation.
findings.fixPrompt: Edit `README.md`. In the `## Configuration` section, find the sentence on line 36: `Relative paths resolve under the app content root. The folder is created automatically on startup.` Replace it with: `Relative paths resolve under the app content root. The folder is created automatically on first use (lazily, the first time a file-system operation targets it), so a misconfigured path surfaces on the first request rather than at startup.` Do not change any other text in the section.

findings.title: Copy depth limit (32) and search result cap (500) are undocumented
findings.description: `Services/FileService.cs` enforces two user-visible limits that the README never mentions. (1) `MaxCopyDepth = 32` is NEW to this branch: `CopyDirectory` aborts with `IOException` ("Directory copy depth limit exceeded (possible cycle)") once recursion reaches 32 levels, so a user copying a deeply nested tree gets an error they cannot explain from the docs. (2) `MaxSearchResults = 500` (pre-existing): `Search` silently stops collecting once it has 500 matches, so a broad query can return fewer results than exist with no indication. Both are behavior-affecting and belong in the README so users understand truncated results / copy failures. Add a short `## Limits` subsection documenting both, including the exact error the copy depth limit raises.
findings.fixPrompt:
Edit `README.md`. Insert a new `## Limits` section immediately after the existing `## Configuration` section (before `## Deep Linking`). Use exactly this content:

## Limits

- **Search results are capped at 500 matches.** A recursive search stops collecting once it reaches 500 entries, so very broad queries may return a truncated result set without further indication.
- **Directory copies are capped at 32 levels of nesting.** Copying a tree deeper than 32 levels aborts with an error (`IOException`: "Directory copy depth limit exceeded (possible cycle)") to guard against stack overflow on pathological or self-referential trees.

Do not modify any other section.

findings.title: Verification checklist omits `path` and `parent` from the browse response
findings.description: The Final End-to-End Verification Checklist item 5 reads: `GET /api/files/browse?path=` — returns JSON with `entries`, `folderCount`, `fileCount`, and `totalSize`. The actual response (see `Models/FileSystemModels.cs` `BrowseResultDto` and `src/api.ts` `BrowseResult`) also includes `path` (the listed folder's relative path) and `parent` (the normalized parent path, or `null` at the root). A reviewer following the checklist would think two fields are missing when the API is working correctly. Update the checklist to list the full response shape.
findings.fixPrompt: Edit `README.md`. In the `## Final End-to-End Verification Checklist` section, find item 5 on line 64: `5. **\`GET /api/files/browse?path=\`** — returns JSON with \`entries\`, \`folderCount\`, \`fileCount\`, and \`totalSize\`.` Replace it with: `5. **\`GET /api/files/browse?path=\`** — returns JSON with \`path\`, \`parent\`, \`entries\`, \`folderCount\`, \`fileCount\`, and \`totalSize\`.` Leave all other checklist items unchanged.

findings.title: Development diary has no entry for the refactor/hardening work
findings.description: `dev-diary.md` is the project's development log and currently has only two entries (harness generation; human review with embedded mode). This branch performed substantial additional work — splitting the `src/app.ts` monolith into focused modules under `src/app/`, restructuring the test suite into per-domain files, hardening `FileService` (lazy directory creation, copy-depth guard, idempotent delete, TOCTOU elimination), refactoring `FilesController` with a shared `Execute`/`ExecuteAsync` fault translator, and adding an ESLint flat config — none of which is recorded. The diary should reflect this work so the development history is accurate.
findings.fixPrompt:
Edit `dev-diary.md`. Append a new numbered entry (item 3) after the existing item 2. Use exactly this content:

3. Refactor and hardening pass: split the `src/app.ts` monolith into focused modules under `src/app/` (context, dom-builders, render-browse, render-search, render-orchestrator, toolbar-handlers) and reorganized the test suite into per-domain files. Hardened `FileService` (lazy home-directory creation via a thread-safe `Lazy`, idempotent delete without a TOCTOU check, a 32-level copy-depth guard, single-pass browse enumeration) and refactored `FilesController` to translate well-known file-system faults to `400 Bad Request` through shared `Execute`/`ExecuteAsync` helpers. Added an ESLint flat config for the TypeScript frontend and build scripts.

Do not edit the existing entries 1 or 2.
